key_findings: Both tools converge on git worktrees as the isolation primitive — this is the de-facto standard for parallel agent execution
Neither tool supports programmatic cross-workspace coordination (supervisor → worker agent routing) — this is the primary gap for OpenSwarm
Conductor is Mac-only, closed-source, GUI-native; no tmux/zellij; supports only Claude Code + Codex; strong UX polish but limited ecosystem
Vibe Kanban is open-source (Bloop AI), cross-platform, browser-based; agent-agnostic (10+ agents); adds kanban board as the coordination layer; no terminal/TUI mode
Conductor's conductor.json pattern (repo-committed setup/run scripts) is a reusable design primitive for bootstrapping workspaces reproducibly
Vibe Kanban exposes its own MCP server (npx vibe-kanban --mcp), enabling meta-orchestration where external agents can create/manage workspaces via MCP tools
Neither tool uses tmux or zellij — isolation is purely git-worktree-based with each tool managing its own UI/process layer
Conductor has checkpoints (mid-session rollback); Vibe Kanban does not — git history is the only rollback in VK
Both tools require human-authored task descriptions / issues; neither auto-decomposes a high-level spec into parallelizable sub-tasks
Security lesson from Conductor: GitHub OAuth (broad permissions) triggered backlash; fine-grained GitHub App auth is required from day one
The shared gap most relevant to OpenSwarm: no DAG-style task dependency, no supervisor-agent model, no structured output validation, no programmatic agent assignment
